content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 1 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 1 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 1:1 -- "Paulos / Paul" and "Christos Yeshua / Christ Jesus" (naming conventions)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Paulos, called apostle of Christos Yeshua through [the] will of God, and Sosthenes the brother" | Preserves Greek and Hebrew name forms; retains article markers in brackets |
| LOGOS | "Paul, called to be an apostle of Christ Jesus by the will of God, and our brother Sosthenes" | Anglicizes names; supplies implied "to be" for smooth English |
| PNEUMA | "This letter is from Paul, chosen by the will of God to be an apostle of Christ Jesus, and from our brother Sosthenes." | Restructures as epistolary introduction; adds "This letter is from"; changes "called" to "chosen" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering ("Paul, called to be an apostle of Christ Jesus by the will of God, and our brother Sosthenes"). Anglicized names serve the English reader; "called to be" preserves the Greek participial sense better than PNEUMA's "chosen." EMET's transliterated forms (Paulos, Christos Yeshua) are preserved in the literal edition for reference.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's transliterations retained as study apparatus. PNEUMA's restructuring rejected as too free for the consensus text.

---

## 1:2 -- Structural expansion and "saints" vs. "holy people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to the assembly of God, the [one] being in Korinthos, having been sanctified in Christos Yeshua, called holy [ones]..." | Word-for-word rendering; "assembly" for ekklesia; "holy ones" for hagioi |
| LOGOS | "To the church of God that is in Corinth, to those sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints..." | Formal equivalent; "church" for ekklesia; "saints" for hagioi |
| PNEUMA | "I am writing to God's church in Corinth, to you who have been called by God to be his own holy people. He made you holy by means of Christ Jesus..." | Expands significantly; adds "I am writing"; "holy people" for hagioi; reorders clauses |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Church" is the standard English equivalent for ekklesia in this context. "Saints" is more concise than "holy people" and carries traditional theological weight. PNEUMA's expansion, while clarifying, adds interpretive material not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "assembly" noted as a valid alternative that avoids anachronistic ecclesiastical connotations.

---

## 1:4 -- "grace of God" vs. "gracious gifts"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I give thanks to the God of me always concerning you, upon the grace of God, the [one] having been given to you in Christos Yeshua" | Literal rendering of preposition epi + dative |
| LOGOS | "I give thanks to my God always for you because of the grace of God that was given you in Christ Jesus" | "because of" interprets the epi construction |
| PNEUMA | "I always thank my God for you and for the gracious gifts he has given you, now that you belong to Christ Jesus" | "gracious gifts" interprets charis as concrete gifts rather than grace in general; adds "now that you belong to" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Grace of God" preserves the singular charis, which in context refers to God's comprehensive grace (further specified in vv.5-7), not merely discrete gifts. PNEUMA's "gracious gifts" is interpretively plausible but narrows the meaning prematurely.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's interpretation noted as a valid secondary reading.

---

## 1:6 -- "testimony about/of Christ" and "confirmed among/in you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "just as the testimony of the Christos was confirmed in you" | Genitive "of the Christos" (subjective or objective); "in you" for en hymin |
| LOGOS | "even as the testimony about Christ was confirmed among you" | Interprets genitive as objective ("about Christ"); "among you" for en hymin |
| PNEUMA | "This confirms that what I told you about Christ is true." | Complete restructuring; makes Paul the speaker; eliminates passive voice |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering with slight modification ("the testimony about Christ was confirmed among you"). "About Christ" correctly interprets the objective genitive. "Among you" better captures the communal sense of en hymin. PNEUMA's rendering loses the passive divine action implied in "was confirmed."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "in you" noted as a legitimate alternative.

---

## 1:7 -- "revealing" vs. "revelation" vs. "return"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "awaiting the revelation of the Lord of us Yeshua Christos" | apokalypsin rendered literally as "revelation" |
| LOGOS | "as you wait for the revealing of our Lord Jesus Christ" | "revealing" as verbal noun preserves dynamic sense |
| PNEUMA | "as you eagerly wait for the return of our Lord Jesus Christ" | "return" interprets the apokalypsis as Second Coming |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "revealing." It preserves the Greek apokalypsin without importing the specific theological interpretation of "return" (PNEUMA) while being more natural in English than "revelation" (EMET) in this context.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "return" is theologically correct but interpretively narrower than the Greek warrants.

---

## 1:8 -- "sustain/confirm" and "guiltless/blameless"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who also will confirm you until [the] end, blameless in the day of the Lord of us Yeshua Christos" | bebaioo as "confirm"; anegkletous as "blameless" |
| LOGOS | "who will sustain you to the end, guiltless in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ" | bebaioo as "sustain"; anegkletous as "guiltless" |
| PNEUMA | "He will keep you strong to the end so that you will be free from all blame on the day when our Lord Jesus Christ returns" | bebaioo as "keep you strong"; anegkletous expanded to "free from all blame"; adds "returns" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Sustain" captures the ongoing supportive action of bebaioo better than "confirm" (which suggests a one-time act). "Guiltless" for anegkletous is a legal term meaning "unindictable," which LOGOS captures well.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "confirm" and "blameless" noted as valid alternatives.

---

## 1:10 -- "appeal/urge" and "judgment/purpose" and "brothers/brothers and sisters"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And I urge you, brothers, through the name of the Lord...that you be restored in the same mind and in the same purpose" | parakaleo as "urge"; katartizo as "restored"; gnome as "purpose" |
| LOGOS | "I appeal to you, brothers...that you be united in the same mind and the same judgment" | parakaleo as "appeal"; katartizo as "united"; gnome as "judgment" |
| PNEUMA | "I appeal to you, dear brothers and sisters, by the authority of our Lord Jesus Christ, to live in harmony...united in thought and purpose" | Adds "dear" and "sisters"; "authority" for "name"; "live in harmony" for "say the same thing" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Appeal" is slightly more formal and appropriate for the rhetorical context. "Judgment" for gnome captures the deliberative sense. EMET's "restored" (katartizo literally means "to mend, make complete") is a valuable alternative that captures the Greek's implication that something broken is being repaired.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "restored" noted for its lexical precision. PNEUMA's "brothers and sisters" acknowledged as reflecting inclusive intent of adelphoi but not adopted for the consensus text, which follows formal equivalence.

---

## 1:12 -- "I follow" vs. "I am of"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am of Paulos," and "I of Apollos," and "I of Kephas," and "I of Christos." | Literal genitive ("of") preserving possessive construction |
| LOGOS | "I follow Paul," or "I follow Apollos," or "I follow Cephas," or "I follow Christ." | Dynamic rendering of possessive genitive as allegiance |
| PNEUMA | "I am a follower of Paul." Others are saying, "I follow Apollos," or "I follow Peter," or "I follow only Christ." | Mixes forms; adds "only" before Christ; uses "Peter" for Kephas |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "I follow" naturally conveys the factional allegiance implied by the genitive. PNEUMA's addition of "only" before Christ is interpretive (the Greek does not distinguish the Christ-party claim with any qualifier). EMET's "I am of" is lexically precise but stilted in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "only" rejected as an interpretive addition. PNEUMA's use of "Peter" for Kephas noted -- SOPHIA retains "Cephas" as Paul's consistent usage.

---

## 1:17 -- "preach the gospel / proclaim good news" and "eloquent wisdom / wisdom of speech"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to proclaim good news, not in wisdom of speech, lest the cross of the Christos be emptied" | euangelizesthai as "proclaim good news"; sophia logou as "wisdom of speech"; kenoo as "be emptied" (no object) |
| LOGOS | "to preach the gospel, and not with words of eloquent wisdom, lest the cross of Christ be emptied of its power" | "preach the gospel"; inverts as "eloquent wisdom"; adds "of its power" |
| PNEUMA | "to preach the Good News -- and not with clever and eloquent speeches, so that the cross of Christ would not lose its power" | "Good News"; "clever and eloquent speeches" expands; "lose its power" for kenoo |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering with modification: "not with words of eloquent wisdom, lest the cross of Christ be emptied of its power." The addition "of its power" makes the kenoo clause intelligible in English. EMET's bare "be emptied" is more literal but less clear. PNEUMA's "clever and eloquent speeches" over-translates.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The supplied "of its power" is an interpretive clarification warranted by context.

---

## 1:18 -- "folly/foolishness/foolish" and "perishing/headed for destruction"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For the word of the cross is foolishness to the [ones] perishing, but to us the [ones] being saved it is [the] power of God" | moria as "foolishness"; apollymi participle as "perishing" |
| LOGOS | "For the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God" | moria as "folly"; same participial rendering |
| PNEUMA | "The message of the cross is foolish to those who are headed for destruction! But we who are being saved know it is the very power of God." | moria as "foolish" (adjective shift); "headed for destruction" for apollymi; adds "very" and exclamation mark |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Folly" is a more precise English equivalent for moria (a noun) than PNEUMA's adjectival shift to "foolish." "Perishing" (LOGOS/EMET) is more faithful than "headed for destruction" (PNEUMA), which implies a future state rather than an ongoing process.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:19 -- "discernment/understanding/intelligence" for synesin

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the understanding of the understanding [ones] I will set aside" | synesin as "understanding"; atheteo as "set aside" |
| LOGOS | "the discernment of the discerning I will thwart" | synesin as "discernment"; atheteo as "thwart" |
| PNEUMA | "discard the intelligence of the intelligent" | synesin as "intelligence"; atheteo as "discard" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Discernment" captures the evaluative sense of synesis. "Thwart" for atheteo conveys active divine opposition better than "set aside" (EMET, too mild) or "discard" (PNEUMA, implies disposal rather than opposition).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:20 -- "debater" and structural differences

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Where [is the] wise? Where [is the] scribe? Where [is the] debater of this age?" | Preserves three rhetorical questions with article markers |
| LOGOS | "Where is the one who is wise? Where is the scribe? Where is the debater of this age?" | Expands first question for English clarity |
| PNEUMA | "So where does this leave the philosophers, the scholars, and the world's brilliant debaters?" | Completely restructures; adds "brilliant"; changes "scribe" to "scholars"; "wise" to "philosophers" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. It preserves Paul's rhetorical tricolon while being clear in English. PNEUMA's "philosophers, scholars, brilliant debaters" over-interprets and adds the evaluative "brilliant" not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:21 -- "folly of what we preach / foolish preaching"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "through the foolishness of the proclamation to save the [ones] believing" | moria tou kerygmatos as "foolishness of the proclamation" |
| LOGOS | "through the folly of what we preach to save those who believe" | "folly of what we preach" -- renders kerygma as verbal phrase |
| PNEUMA | "he has used our foolish preaching to save those who believe" | "foolish preaching" shifts moria from noun to adjective; adds "our" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "The folly of what we preach" correctly makes moria the noun (the content is "folly" by worldly standards). PNEUMA's "foolish preaching" incorrectly suggests the preaching method is foolish rather than the message being perceived as folly.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:22 -- "Jews/Ioudaioi" naming decision

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Ioudaioi ask for signs and Greeks seek wisdom" | Preserves transliterated Ioudaioi |
| LOGOS | "Jews demand signs and Greeks seek wisdom" | Standard English "Jews" |
| PNEUMA | "It is foolish to the Jews, who ask for signs from heaven. And it is foolish to the Greeks, who seek human wisdom." | Expands with "from heaven" and "human"; adds "it is foolish to" frame |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's concise rendering. The debate over "Jews" vs. "Ioudaioi" is significant (EMET preserves the Greek to avoid anachronistic connotations), but for the consensus text, the standard English "Jews" is used with a note acknowledging the translation issue. PNEUMA's additions ("from heaven," "human") are interpretive expansions.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's transliteration policy noted as a valid scholarly approach.

---

## 1:23 -- "stumbling block" and "Gentiles/nations"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to Ioudaioi a stumbling block and to nations foolishness" | ethnesin as "nations" |
| LOGOS | "a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Gentiles" | ethnesin as "Gentiles" |
| PNEUMA | "the Jews are offended and the Gentiles say it's all nonsense" | Active voice restructuring; "offended" for skandalon; "nonsense" for moria |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Gentiles" is the standard English equivalent for ethne in this theological context. PNEUMA's active restructuring ("Jews are offended") loses the skandalon ("stumbling block") metaphor, which is theologically significant.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "nations" is lexically precise but less conventional in this context.

---

## 1:25 -- "men/human plans/human strength"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the foolishness of God is wiser than the men, and the weakness of God is stronger than the men" | ton anthropon with article, "the men" |
| LOGOS | "the foolishness of God is wiser than men, and the weakness of God is stronger than men" | Generic "men" without article |
| PNEUMA | "This foolish plan of God is wiser than the wisest of human plans, and God's weakness is stronger than the greatest of human strength" | Major expansion; adds "wisest of human plans" and "greatest of human strength" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The generic comparison ("wiser than men") captures the universal scope. PNEUMA's expansion ("wisest of human plans," "greatest of human strength") intensifies the rhetoric but adds superlatives not present in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:26 -- "worldly standards / according to flesh"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not many wise according to flesh, not many powerful, not many well-born" | kata sarka as "according to flesh"; eugeneis as "well-born" |
| LOGOS | "not many of you were wise according to worldly standards, not many were powerful, not many were of noble birth" | kata sarka as "worldly standards"; eugeneis as "noble birth" |
| PNEUMA | "few of you were wise in the world's eyes or powerful or wealthy when God called you" | "few" instead of "not many"; eugeneis as "wealthy" (mistranslation); adds "when God called you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Worldly standards" effectively interprets kata sarka for English readers. PNEUMA's rendering of eugeneis as "wealthy" is a mistranslation -- the word means "well-born" or "of noble birth," referring to social class by lineage, not wealth. PNEUMA's "few" for "not many" slightly shifts the emphasis.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "wealthy" flagged as an error; eugeneis means "noble birth."

---

## 1:29 -- "no human being / all flesh / no one"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "so that all flesh might not boast before God" | pasa sarx as "all flesh" |
| LOGOS | "so that no human being might boast in the presence of God" | pasa sarx as "no human being" (with negation restructured) |
| PNEUMA | "As a result, no one can ever boast in the presence of God" | "no one" simplifies; adds "ever" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "No human being" captures the Hebraic pasa sarx ("all flesh") while restructuring the negation for English clarity. PNEUMA's "no one" is acceptable but loses the embodied "flesh" reference. EMET's "all flesh" is the most literal but awkward in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:30 -- "became to us wisdom" vs. "made him to be wisdom itself"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who became wisdom to us from God, and righteousness and sanctification and redemption" | Straightforward rendering of egenethe |
| LOGOS | "who became to us wisdom from God, righteousness and sanctification and redemption" | Nearly identical to EMET |
| PNEUMA | "For our benefit God made him to be wisdom itself. Christ made us right with God; he made us pure and holy, and he freed us from sin." | Breaks into multiple sentences; adds "itself"; converts abstract nouns to active verbs |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The fourfold attribution (wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, redemption) should remain as a unified christological statement. PNEUMA's expansion into separate sentences with active verbs is interpretively valid but loses the compressed creedal quality of the original.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 1:31 -- "Let the one who boasts" vs. "If you want to boast"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The [one] boasting, let him boast in [the] Lord" | Participial construction preserved |
| LOGOS | "Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord" | English relative clause |
| PNEUMA | "If you want to boast, boast only about the Lord" | Conditional restructuring; adds "only"; changes "in" to "about" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The jussive "Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord" preserves the Jeremiah 9:24 citation form. PNEUMA's conditional ("if you want to") and added "only" are interpretive additions.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| EMET retains Greek/Hebrew name forms vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA anglicize | vv.1, 2, 12, 22, 23 | Paulos/Paul, Ioudaioi/Jews, Kephas/Cephas |
| PNEUMA adds interpretive expansions not in Greek | vv.1, 2, 7, 17, 20, 25, 26, 30 | "This letter is from," "return" for apokalypsis, "brilliant debaters" |
| PNEUMA restructures sentences into active/dynamic forms | vv.6, 18, 23, 30, 31 | Passive to active voice, abstract nouns to verbs |
| EMET uses bracket-heavy literal rendering vs. LOGOS smooth formal | Throughout | Article markers, supplied words |
| LOGOS and SOPHIA consensus nearly identical | Most verses | LOGOS served as primary base for consensus |
| PNEUMA mistranslation | v.26 | eugeneis rendered as "wealthy" instead of "noble birth" |
| Word choice divergence for key theological terms | vv.7, 8, 17, 19, 21 | apokalypsis, anegkletous, kenoo, synesis, kerygma |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** In Chapter 1, LOGOS and SOPHIA's consensus text align very closely, with LOGOS serving as the effective base translation. EMET diverges primarily in its policy of retaining transliterated Greek/Hebrew forms and ultra-literal syntax. PNEUMA diverges most significantly through expansive paraphrasing, sentence restructuring, and occasional interpretive additions not warranted by the Greek text. One translation error was identified in PNEUMA (v.26, eugeneis as "wealthy"). The most substantive theological divergences concern the rendering of apokalypsis (v.7), the kenoo clause (v.17), and the skandalon metaphor (v.23), where PNEUMA's dynamic approach risks losing important nuances preserved by the more formal approaches.

---
